blocks.text: A general method for preparing spirocyclic/cyclic tetraarylmethanes through one-step cyclization of diaryl compounds (diaryl, diarylamine, diaryl ether, and diaryl thioether) with diarylketones using triflimide (HNTf₂) as a catalyst has been developed. The key step involves a Friedel-Crafts reaction. This straightforward synthetic approach features wide substrate scope, mild reaction conditions, a low catalyst amount, good yield, and allows the synthesis of unusual spirocyclic compounds that are otherwise difficult to achieve.

spans.text: A general method for preparing spirocyclic/cyclic tetraarylmethanes through one-step cyclization of diaryl compounds (diaryl, diarylamine, diaryl ether, and diaryl
thioether) with diarylketones using triflimide (HNTf₂) as a catalyst has been developed. The key step involves a Friedel-Crafts reaction. This straightforward synthetic
approach features wide substrate scope, mild reaction conditions, a low catalyst amount, good yield, and allows the synthesis of unusual spirocyclic compounds that
are otherwise difficult to achieve.

blocks.text: Spirocyclic/cyclic tetraarylmethanes (SAMs, Scheme 1) possess 9 0 ^ { ^ { \circ } } - twisted rigid conformation and steric hindrance due to the \mathsf { s p } ^ { 3 } . - hy- bridized carbon, which plays a pivotal role in suppressing the dye ag- gregation, enhancing their film-forming properties, photo/thermal stability, and optoelectronic performance [1–6]. The molecular struc- tures of SAMs provide crucial building blocks in the preparation of many organic functional materials such as thermally activated delayed fluo- rescent emitters, organic field-effect transistors, light-emitting diodes, solar cells, hole transport materials, fluorescence probes and others [4–13].

blocks.text: The conventional synthesis of SAMs still relies on complex, multistep synthesis routes and involves Grignard reagents or lithium reagents (Scheme 2) [2,5,9,14]. In general, the starting biaryl compound A (such as biaryl, diarylamine, diaryl ether, diaryl thioether) is metalated indi- rectly via stepwise halogenation and metalation or metalated directly. The metalation reaction generally requires harsh reaction conditions, such as an anhydrous and oxygen - free environment. The formed metalated intermediate B or Ar₁MgBr/ Ar₁Li is then coupled with dia- rylketone/fluorenone to form the corresponding alcohol E or F. Finally, the desired target molecule TM1 or TM2 is obtained through a Frie- del− Crafts reaction under the catalytic action of an acid. For the ^ { 9 , 9 ^ { \prime } } - diarylfluorene compound with two identical substituents at the \mathrm { C _ { 9 } } po- sition, the compound (TM3) can be prepared in one step [2]. However, the reaction requires a relatively high temperature, and it can only be used to introduce electron - rich aryl groups as substituents at the \mathrm { C _ { 9 } } position of fluorene (Scheme 3, route III). The active N–H of

blocks.text: In 2006, Huang and colleagues pioneered a simple yet highly effec- tive one – pot synthesis method for spiro(fluorene - 9,9′-xanthene) (SFX) [4]. This approach utilized fluorenone and phenol as reactants in the presence of \mathrm { M e S O _ { 3 } H } (catalyst).

blocks.text: M. Bhanuchandra et al. employed diarylsulfone and diarylmethane as starting materials and used bis(trimethylsilyl)potassium amide as a catalyst to synthesize fluorene - based spirocyclic tetraarylmethanes in a single step [1]. Wang and Yang proposed a related synthesis strategy for spiro - acridans from fluorenones, with p - TsOH serving as the activator [9]. In 2024, Hirano et al. reported a Tf₂O - mediated direct dehydrative coupling of biaryls and fluorenones [5]. In summary, each of the aforementioned one - pot methods has the disadvantage of a narrow substrate scope, along with other limitations, such as the need for high - temperature reactions [4,9], the requirement for specific [1] or large quantities of catalysts [5], or the occurrence of low yields [3].

blocks.text: Triflimide (HNTf₂), also known as bis(trifluoromethanesulfonyl) imide, is a commercially accessible and highly versatile super Brønsted acid [15]. Its exceptional acidity and excellent compatibility with organic solvents have made it a valuable catalyst, promoter, and addi- tive in various organic reactions [16–20]. Unlike TfOH (a corrosive, fuming liquid), triflimide is much more user-friendly. A pioneering example of using triflimide to catalyze a Friedel-Crafts alkylation

blocks.text: reaction was first demonstrated in 1996 by Yamamoto and co-workers [21]. Subsequently, a series of Friedel- Crafts and related reactions catalyzed by triflimide have been reported [22–25]. However, no syn- thetic reaction of spirocyclic compounds catalyzed by triflimide has been investigated.

blocks.text: As can be seen from Table 1, the yield reaches the highest when the feed ratio of 1a to 2a is 1.1:1 and the catalyst amount (relative to 2a) is 1 0 \mathrm { m o l } \% . For example, by employing 1.1 equiv. of 1a, 1.0 equiv. of 2a and 0.1 equiv. of HNTf₂ in 1,4 - dioxane, the reaction was carried out at 8 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } for 6 h. SPF1 was obtained in 8 9 ~ \% yield (Table 1, entrys 1–3).

blocks.text: The catalyst was screened using different types of acids, including common organic acids (such as formic acid, acetic acid, trifluoroacetic acid, p -toluenesulfonic acid, succinic acid, and benzenesulfonic acid) and common inorganic acids (such as hydrochloric acid, nitric acid, sulfuric acid, phosphoric acid, hydrobromic acid, phosphorous acid, sulfurous acid, and tetrafluoroboric acid), as well as catalyst Cat.I，Cat. II and Cat.III (Table 1). Under the given conditions ( 8 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } , 6 \mathrm { h } , catalyst amount = 1 0 \mathrm { m o l \% } ), only HNTf₂ and its analogues Cat.I, Cat.II were able to catalyze the reaction, whereas common organic acids, inorganic acids, and Cat.III had no catalytic effect (Table 1). Compared with its analogues (Cat.I, Cat.II), HNTf₂ exhibited better catalytic performance.

blocks.text: With \mathrm { H N T f } _ { 2 } as a promising catalyst, the solvent effect has been investigated. The reaction was conducted in a variety of solvents, including both polar and non-polar ones, such as 1,4-dioxane, DMF, DMSO, acetic acid, toluene, cyclohexane, and 1,2-dichloroethane. The results indicate that all these solvents are effective, and the polar sol- vents exhibit even better performance.

blocks.text: reaction mixture was heated at 8 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } for 6 h. After that, the solution was poured into a beaker containing a large amount of water. The crude product was collected by vacuum filtration, then purified by silica gel column chromatography (using petroleum ether/ethyl acetate, 10–5:1, v/v) to yield a solid. If the raw material contains functional groups (e.g., amino groups) that are easily oxidized, the reaction needs to be carried out under nitrogen protection.

blocks.text: The protocol exhibits notable functional group tolerance. Various substituents such as methyl \left( \mathrm { - C H } _ { 3 } \right) , bromo \mathbf { \left( - B r \right) } , hydroxyl (–OH), and amino (–NH₂) do not impede the successful completion of the reaction. This advantage is critical for advancing applications of SAM-based compounds, as these functional groups serve as versatile handles for further chemical modifications, molecular recognition, and interface engineering.

blocks.text: Based on literature precedents [3,10] and the verification of various substrates, a plausible reaction mechanism is proposed (Scheme 3). The diaryl compounds, such as diphenyl, diaryla-mine, diaryl ether, and diaryl thioether, that bear electron-donating or electron-withdrawing groups can form the corresponding spirocyclic/cyclic tetraaryl- methanes. Among them, electron-rich substrates exhibit better reaction performance (Table 2), which reflects the electrophilic substitution re- action mechanism of this process. For the proposed reaction pathway, we have confirmed its possibility through computational methods aimed at obtaining mechanistic details and energy distribution (Scheme 4). At the beginning of the reaction, the carbonyl oxygen of fluorenone is protonated by the proton provided by \mathrm { H N T f } _ { 2 } , forming a carbocation

blocks.text: intermediate IM1. The energy barrier of its transition state (TS1) is 43.16 kcal/mol. Subsequently, the carbocation intermediate is attacked by the \alpha -position carbon of the benzene ring in diphenylamine, resulting in the formation of an unstable compound IM2. The energy barrier of its transition state (TS2) is 32.36 kcal/mol. Then, a proton in the envi- ronment attacks the hydroxyl group on compound IM2, causing the hydroxyl group to leave as water and forming a new carbocation in- termediate IM3. The energy barrier of its transition state (TS3) is 5.19 kcal/mol. Finally, the benzene ring carbon in diphenylamine nucleo- philically attacks the intermediate.

blocks.text: IM3 to yield the final product IM4, with the energy barrier of its transition state (TS4) being 2.39 kcal/mol. Through calculations, we have verified the feasibility of the reaction pathway. In addition, the reactions between diaryl compounds (diarylamine, diaryl ether) and dialkyl ketones (such as 1,3-dibromopropan-2-one) were investigated and good yields were obtained (Supporting Information).

blocks.text: In conclusion, we have developed a general one-step synthetic method for spirocyclic/cyclic tetraarylmethanes. These reactions are operationally straightforward under mild conditions. The protocol ex- hibits notable functional group tolerance, which is conducive to exploring the potential applications of spirocyclic/cyclic tetraaryl- methanes in the field of organic electronics. Experiments have demon- strated that this strategy is applicable to reactions on a gram scale or above, which holds significant importance in material synthesis and industrial production.
